what_it_does: Declares an operator-posture overlay for the tool face: a per-tool map that marks which tools a trusted local operator may run with posture 'safe', widening the operator door beyond the tighter remote connector posture while leaving absent tools to fall back to the remote posture.
observations: It is a separate registry from remote_exposure.json by design, serving a different threat model (trusted local operator vs untrusted remote client).
Every listed tool currently has only 'operator_posture': 'safe'; no locked or hazard-confirm entries appear in this file.
The file explicitly states that absent rows or an absent file cause the remote posture to govern, enforcing a fail-closed default.
It documents a phased plan: phase 1 widens reads now; phase 2 will widen consequential writes only after a unified-floor operator-token attribution mechanism is live.
A bridge gate is mentioned that reads operator_posture from here and has precedence over remote_posture.
Explicitly denied verbs from remote_exposure.json are excluded from this door and require dedicated gated routes.
standouts: Dual-door security model: separate overlays for operator face and remote connector on the same tool face.
Degrade-clean semantics: missing file or missing row falls back to the stricter remote posture.
Phase-gated widening: reads are opened now, writes are deliberately deferred.
All current entries are read-oriented observational tools.
conventions: Uses a versioned JSON schema with extensive metadata notes.
Declares posture as a simple string enum per tool.
Follows a fail-closed default by not auto-allowing unlisted tools.
Separates concerns by threat model rather than merging operator and remote rules.
Documents rationale and future phases inline.
concerns: Only one posture value ('safe') is used, so the file gives no visible gradation for riskier-but-allowed tools.
The planned phase 2 write widening is not yet present, so the file is intentionally incomplete for writes.
operator_posture winning over remote_posture could become risky if the overlay is widened carelessly later.
There are no machine-readable side-effect or tool-category fields to mechanically enforce the read-vs-write distinction.
The bridge gate consumption logic is described only in prose, not in a machine-readable schema.
notes: This is the operator-side allowlist for low-risk read tools, designed to stay fail-closed for anything not explicitly listed.
questions: What are the exact allowed values and semantics for operator_posture beyond 'safe', 'locked', and 'hazard-confirm'?
How does the bridge gate merge this overlay with remote_exposure.json at runtime?
What criteria determine whether a tool is classified as a pure no-side-effect read versus a consequential write?
When will the unified-floor operator-token attribution mechanism go live so phase 2 writes can be classified?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file's stated purpose is to widen operator posture for safe read-only tools in phase 1, and it contains only 'safe' entries for read-oriented tools while deferring consequential writes.
apparent_themes: separation of operator and remote threat models
least privilege for untrusted remote clients
intention_signals: It is trying to give a trusted local operator broader, friction-free access to observational tools without weakening the remote connector's tight default-deny posture, using a separate overlay and staged rollout to avoid ungated write paths.
novelty: The dual-door overlay model: two separate posture registries for the same tool face keyed to different trust domains (remote client vs local operator), with explicit degrade-clean semantics and a phase-gated widening plan.
contribution: It contributes a scoped, operator-specific posture layer that lets low-risk read tools run freely for a trusted local operator while preserving a fail-closed fallback to the remote connector posture for any unclassified or missing tool.
summary_for_embedding: A JSON operator posture overlay that marks selected read-only MCP tools as safe for a trusted local operator, with fail-closed fallback to the remote connector posture for unlisted tools.
intention_for_embedding: To widen the operator face door for observational tools in phase 1 while keeping consequential writes and high-risk verbs gated until a unified operator-token attribution floor is ready.
